---
title: "Human Activity Recognition (CASAS) Executive Summary"
subtitle: "Ambient sensor windows → activity predictions (XGBoost baseline)"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: false
    page-layout: full
---

<!-- REMOVE_FOR_FINAL:BEGIN mockup -->
::: {.callout-important title="DRAFT NOTE (remove for final)" .remove-for-final}
This is a mock-up executive summary. It is meant to be readable and professional first, and fully sourced/complete second. We can tighten claims and swap in final metrics once you decide which target and evaluation protocol you want to report.
:::
<!-- REMOVE_FOR_FINAL:END mockup -->

<div class="es-metrics">
  <div class="es-card">
    <div class="kicker">Targets considered</div>
    <div class="value">41 specific / 11 generalized</div>
    <div class="note">Generalized labels reduce sparsity and imbalance.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="es-card">
    <div class="kicker">Best quick baseline (macro-F1)</div>
    <div class="value">0.720</div>
    <div class="note">For <code>activity_general</code> (row-level CV).</div>
  </div>
  <div class="es-card">
    <div class="kicker">Dataset</div>
    <div class="value">30 homes</div>
    <div class="note">Each row is a sliding window of 30 sensor events.</div>
  </div>
</div>

## Executive Summary

This project builds a multiclass classifier to recognize daily activities in smart homes from ambient sensor streams (motion, doors, light, etc.). Each training instance is a **feature vector computed over a sliding window of 30 sensor events**, and the model predicts either a fine-grained activity label (41-class) or a generalized activity category (11-class).

The clearest takeaway from early results is that **generalized labels are substantially more learnable and more stable**, improving macro-F1, weighted-F1, and accuracy in a quick baseline comparison. If the goal is dependable activity recognition rather than strict sub-activity identification, the generalized target is the right default.

## Problem & Goal

**Problem:** Given a feature vector summarizing the most recent sensor activity in a home, predict what activity is occurring.

**Goal:** Produce a model that is accurate enough for downstream use (monitoring, summarization, alerting, or analytics) and robust enough to run under local compute constraints.

## Data & Labels

- Source: CASAS smart home ambient sensor dataset (homes `csh101`–`csh130`).
- Instances: sliding window of 30 sensor events, summarized into numeric features.
- Features: 36 numeric features per row (plus label columns).
- Labels:
  - `activity`: specific activities (41 classes in current pipeline).
  - `activity_general`: 11 generalized categories (Hygiene, Leisure, Meal_Prep, etc.).

::: {.callout-note}
Generalization is performed by mapping multiple specific labels into a smaller set (e.g., `Cook_Breakfast`, `Cook_Lunch`, `Cook_Dinner` → `Meal_Prep`).
:::

## Modeling Approach

### Baseline Model

We use **XGBoost (gradient boosted decision trees)** because it:
- handles heterogeneous numeric features well without normalization,
- scales to large tabular datasets efficiently (`tree_method="hist"`),
- supports multiclass probabilities (needed for log loss and calibration).

### Primary Metric

For reporting, we focus on **macro-F1** because the class distribution is imbalanced and we want each class to matter. We treat accuracy and weighted-F1 as secondary diagnostics.

## Implementation (Selected Code)

These are short excerpts that reflect the current pipeline used in `notebooks/exploratory.ipynb` and `scripts/train_final.py`.

### Label Cleanup + Generalization (Polars)

```python
# Normalize odd labels like r1.Sleep / r2.Dress and add a generalized target column.
df_home = (
    pl.scan_csv(path)
    .with_columns(pl.col("activity").str.replace(r"^r[12]\.", "").alias("activity"))
    .filter(pl.col("activity") != "Other_Activity")
    .with_columns(pl.col("activity").replace(general_map).alias("activity_general"))
)
```

### Memory-Safe Sampling (Per Home)

```python
# Collect per-home then sample, to avoid materializing all homes at once.
df_home_sampled = df_home.collect().sample(fraction=0.30, seed=seed, shuffle=True)
```

### Training Commands (XGBoost)

```bash
# Train on generalized labels (recommended default from baseline results)
python scripts/train_final.py --api native --target-column activity_general \
  --sample-fraction 0.05 --n-folds 5 --n-trials 12 --reserve-threads 4 --verbosity 2

# Alternate branch via sklearn wrapper
python scripts/train_final.py --api sklearn --target-column activity_general \
  --sample-fraction 0.05 --n-folds 5 --n-trials 12 --reserve-threads 4 --verbosity 2
```

### Saved Artifacts

```text
models/xgb_native_<target>_f1_macro_<score>_<timestamp>.json
models/meta_native_<target>_f1_macro_<score>_<timestamp>.json
models/xgb_sklearn_<target>_f1_macro_<score>_<timestamp>.pkl
models/meta_sklearn_<target>_f1_macro_<score>_<timestamp>.json
```

## Key Results (Quick Baseline)

The table below compares a lightweight baseline evaluation for the two target choices:

| target | n_classes | cv_f1_macro_mean | cv_f1_macro_std | cv_f1_weighted_mean | cv_accuracy_mean |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| activity_general | 11 | 0.7198 | 0.0156 | 0.7838 | 0.7921 |
| activity | 41 | 0.6566 | 0.0021 | 0.6990 | 0.7083 |

**Interpretation:** Generalized labels improve macro-F1, weighted-F1, and accuracy. This supports training on `activity_general` as the main target if reliability is the priority.

## Metrics (Saved Training Runs)

The table below is auto-generated from the saved run metadata in `models/meta_*.json` (refresh via `python docs/executive_summary/tools/generate_model_metrics_md.py`). It reflects the **primary CV score** used during training-time model selection (currently macro-F1 with 5-fold KFold on the sampled training set). Rows with `N/A` indicate a run that did not produce a valid CV score, but are kept for traceability.

{{< include _includes/model_metrics.md >}}

<!-- REMOVE_FOR_FINAL:BEGIN validation-caveat -->
::: {.callout-warning title="VALIDATION CAVEAT (remove for final)" .remove-for-final}
This baseline uses row-level cross validation, which may overestimate true generalization because the data is created from overlapping sliding windows. A more defensible “unseen home” score would use a grouped split by `home_id`.
:::
<!-- REMOVE_FOR_FINAL:END validation-caveat -->

## Risks, Limitations, and What Would Make It Better

- **Leakage risk:** adjacent windows are highly correlated; random row splits inflate metrics.
- **Cross-home generalization:** models can learn home-specific patterns; holding out homes is a stronger test.
- **Extreme class imbalance:** some specific labels are extremely rare; evaluation variance can be misleading.
- **Interpretability:** boosted trees are interpretable at the feature level, but not causal; confusion matrices matter.

Compensations we can make (and should, if this is a final report):
- Switch evaluation to `GroupKFold` by `home_id` (or leave-one-home-out).
- Report both macro-F1 and log loss, plus a confusion matrix for top activities.
- Consider a two-stage model: generalized prediction first, then specific within a bucket.

## Recommendation

Default to training on **generalized labels** (`activity_general`). If sub-activities are still required, add a second-stage classifier trained only inside each generalized bucket; this keeps the primary model stable and reduces failure modes on rare fine-grained labels.

## Render & Publish

### Local render

From repo root:

```bash
quarto render docs/executive_summary
```

This produces `docs/executive_summary/_site/index.html`.

### GitHub Pages

This repo is configured to render and deploy via GitHub Actions on push (`.github/workflows/quarto-pages.yml`).
